one.en: PARENTING – Final orders made by consent for the mother to have sole-decision making responsibility and for the children to live with the mother – Whe…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Final orders made by consent for the mother to have sole-decision making responsibility and for the children to live with the mother – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer does not oppose the final orders but seeks an opportunity to meet with the children prior to the making of the orders to consider whether there should be an order for the children to attend therapy – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer clearly has confidence in the mother’s sole-decision making responsibility – Where a further delay in finalising the Consent Orders is in no party’s interest – Orders made in accordance with the Consent Orders. FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer made an application for costs fixed in the sum of $7,134.88 – Where the mother opposed an order for costs – Where the father conceded to an order to pay half of the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s costs – Where the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer were afforded the opportunity to provide written submissions to chambers as to costs – Order made for the father to pay half of the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s costs – Further orders made for the outstanding costs dispute as between the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer to be determined in chambers on the papers.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

The parties to these proceedings, namely the mother, the father, the paternal grandparents and the Independent Children’s Lawyer, have, with the exception of the Independent Children’s Lawyer, reached agreement in the terms of a document which will ultimately be marked as Exhibit 1. The terms of that order would bring to an end the litigation before this Court involving the parties and their two children, who are 12 and 15.
I am satisfied that the financial orders represent a just and equitable determination of the parties’ rights pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).
In relation to the parenting orders, the orders provide for the mother to have sole-decision making responsibility, albeit they rather ineloquently describe it as sole-parental responsibility, and for the children to live with the mother. In discussions this morning with the Independent Children’s Lawyer, she indicated that she did not oppose the making of those two orders but otherwise wanted an opportunity to speak to the children to consider whether an order should be made for the children to attend upon some form of therapy.
I am not prepared to delay the making of these orders. The Independent Children’s Lawyer clearly has sufficient confidence in the mother’s sole decision-making responsibility to agree to an order that invests her with that responsibility. In those circumstances, it seems to me that a further delay in the ending of this litigation is in the interests of no one and, accordingly, I propose to make the orders sought, albeit noting the position of the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
As stated, I intend to mark as Exhibit 1 in this application a document described as “Minutes of Order” and I will make orders in terms of paragraphs one through to twenty of that documents.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer seeks an order for the costs of her engagement in the litigation. The sum sought is $7,134.88. She seeks an order that the parents share that cost equally.
Counsel who appears on behalf of the respondent father do not oppose the order as sought by the Independent Children’s lawyer. The solicitor for the mother seeks an opportunity to see how the sum is calculated and thereafter to obtain instructions in relation to the said sum. I propose to give her a short time to do so, but not an indefinite period of time. Accordingly, I propose to make the following orders.
